coredata.dc:title: Determination of acetaldehyde in Chinese Baijiu based on the light-activated oxidase-mimic activity of fluorine-containing carbon dots (dF-CDs)

coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Xin-He
Li, Hong-Wei
Li, Yi
Huang, Zhijiu
Liu, Sha
Wu, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: Acetaldehyde (AH) is defined as a carcinogen, the widely existence in alcoholic beverages has led its determination an essential aspect related to human health. Fluorine doped carbon dots (dF-CDs) is developed, which process light-activated oxidase-mimic property and high stability at acidic condition. Under the UV irradiation, dF-CDs generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), showing absorption peak at 450 nm and strong emission at 577 nm, meanwhile, the produced DAP quenches the original emission of dF-CDs at 455 nm via an inner filter effect (IFE). However, the introduction of AH inhibits such processes efficiently, thereby establishing a colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence method for AH determination. Of note, this dual-responsive method has been used for the accurate AH determination in Chinese Baijiu. Therefore, the developed method not only provide a dual-sensing platform to determine AH, but also extends the applications of CDs in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorine-containing CDs
Dual-responsive optical method
Acetaldehyde determination
Chinese Baijiu
Light-activated oxidase-mimic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric determination of AH
Fluorometric and colorimetric determination of AH in Baijiu samples
Dual-responsive optical model of dF-CDs/OPD for AH determination
Response mechanism of dF-CDs/OPD system to AH
Specific response of dF-CDs to AH
Determination of AH in Chinese Baijiu

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (insert: histogram of particle size distribution), (B) FT-IR, (C) XPS, (D) UV–vis absorption (blue), and fluorescence spectra (λex = 360–400 nm) of dF-CDs, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra of (A) dF-CDs, dF-CDs/OPD, dF-CDs/OPD/AH, OPD; and (B) dF-CDs/TMB, dF-CDs/ABTS, TMB, ABTS with or without UV light, respectively. (C) Steady-state kinetic assay on the catalytic activity of dF-CDs under the irradiation of 365 nm; and (D) the corresponding double reciprocal plots. (E) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the catalytic oxidation of OPD by dF-CDs under the air- or N2-saturated atmosphere. (F) The normalized absorbance intensity of TMB oxidation by dF-CDs under light irradiation by adding different scavengers, respectively.

(A) Comparison of the emission spectrum of dF-CDs (λex = 380 nm) and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP. (B) Emission decay curves of dF-CDs and dF-CDs/OPD at 370 nm in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0). (C) The plot of I 455 for the dF-CDs or I 577/I 455 for the finished dF-CDs/OPD system responding to different concentrations of AH. (D) The liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry assay of the products from dF-CDs/OPD/AH in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the dF-CDs/OPD system responding to various concentrations of AH (0, 10, 30, 40, 50, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200 μM). (B) The linear relationship for AH sensing. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the dF-CDs/OPD system with various concentrations of AH (0, 10, 30, 40, 50, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200 μM). (D) The linear relationship for AH sensing. (E) Fluorescence spectra of dF-CDs/OPD after adding AH or different kinds of 100 μM ester or alcohol (ethyl lactate (EL), ethyl hexanoate (EH), methyl acetate (MA), ethyl caprylate (EC), 3-methyl-1-butanol (MB), 2-methyl-1-propanol (MP), methanol (MeOH), 1-butanol (NBA), phenethyl alcohol (PA), ethanol (EtOH)), respectively; (F) The selectivity of dF-CDs/OPD to AH by monitoring the I 577/I 455.
Schematic illustration for dF-CDs synthesis and the application of it to AH determination, based on the light-activated dual-responsive optical models.
Comparison of the AH content in Baijiu samples determined by the proposed method and GC (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Determination of acetaldehyde in Chinese Baijiu based on the light-activated oxidase-mimic activity of fluorine-containing carbon dots (dF-CDs)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acetaldehyde (AH) is defined as a carcinogen, the widely existence in alcoholic beverages has led its determination an essential aspect related to human health. Fluorine doped carbon dots (dF-CDs) is developed, which process light-activated oxidase-mimic property and high stability at acidic condition. Under the UV irradiation, dF-CDs generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), showing absorption peak at 450 nm and strong emission at 577 nm, meanwhile, the produced DAP quenches the original emission of dF-CDs at 455 nm via an inner filter effect (IFE). However, the introduction of AH inhibits such processes efficiently, thereby establishing a colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence method for AH determination. Of note, this dual-responsive method has been used for the accurate AH determination in Chinese Baijiu. Therefore, the developed method not only provide a dual-sensing platform to determine AH, but also extends the applications of CDs in food analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing platform for acetaldehyde determination in Baijiu was developed using the fluorine-containing carbon dots with light-activated oxidase-mimic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The amount of alcohols, esters, carbonyls and sulfides in alcoholic beverages have great impact on flavor and quality [1]. Among them, aldehydes are the primary carbonyl compounds, while acetaldehyde (AH) is the highest percentage of aldehydes in the alcoholic beverage as Chinese Baijiu [2]. Moderate amount of AH endows Baijiu with unique flavors, thus result in its content an important parameter for quality control and qualify. For example, the high-grade liquors are in general produced by the solid fermentation method [3], which always include a moderate level of AH. On the contrary, the AH level in some low-grade liquors is considerably different from that of the high-grade ones due to the using of liquid fermentation method [4]. Meanwhile, the AH obtained by drinking alcoholic beverages is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) [5]. Therefore, the determination of AH in alcoholic beverage as Chinese Baijiu is not only crucial for quality control but also an essential aspect related to human health. Besides, the aldol condensation reaction between AH and ethanol is easily occur during storage of alcoholic beverages [6]. It is urgently needed to establish a simple and convenient method for the real-time determination of AH.

Currently, several methods have been used for the determination of AH in alcoholic beverages, where high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography (GC) are predominant [1,7,8]. AH can be determined at lower concentration using these analytical methods; however, significant challenges in overcoming the disadvantages as complicated sample pretreatment, expensive instrument and high requirement of skillful expert for operation cannot be overlooked [1,9]. Compared to them, optical methods with merits of low-cost, ultra-high sensitivity and simple instruments have become a convenient and powerful analysis tool [10,11]. However, the problems associated with sensitivity of optical methods have always been prominent as most of them are single-channel readouts [12], whose accuracy and reproducibility possibly to be compromised by the coexisting interferons or environmental factors. Being different, the optical method with multi-channel readouts is considered as a more promising paradigm as which can offer signal outputs in different models to meet requirements of different sensitivity of analysts in different application scenarios [13]. In particular, the results obtained from different models can be verified against each other, thereby effectively improving the accuracy and reliability of determination [14]. Therefore, constructing a more reliable multimodel sensing platforms will be a topic of concern.

Carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (CDs), g-C3N4 quantum dots and MXene quantum dots, both with luminescence and catalytic properties shin lights on establishing multimodel sensing system [15]. As a class of low-dimensional nanomaterials, the core of CDs, either amorphous or crystalline, is essentially comprised of sp2 carbon; while the surface possibly present versatile functional groups [16]. Such structural features endow CDs unique properties, including excellent light harvesting ability, tunable photoluminescence and efficient photo-excited electron transfer, depending on the synthesis methods and surface functional groups. In particular, the photo-catalytic properties of CDs have drawn increasing attention recently [17], which have been proven essentially relying on the photo-generated electrons and holes. The electron transfer of CDs induced by photo-excitation retards the reunite of electron-hole pairs efficiently, finally extending the lifetime of photo-activated electrons and holes in CDs [18,19]. Hence, CDs can be used as efficient photo-catalysts to improve either oxidation reaction or reduction reaction. For example, Aggarwal et al. fabricated photoactive CDs by charring bitter apple peel, which were used as photo-catalysts for the photo-degradation of crystal violet (CV) under sunlight [20]. Saini et al. prepared CDs through microwave-assisted method by using polyethylene glycol, imidazole, and thiourea as carbon sources, which was used as a sunlight activated material for the reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) [21]. Besides, Chang et al. synthesized CDs from coal pitch in a simple and scalable method, under light irradiation which reduced quinone through preferentially captured the photoexcited electrons of CDs [22].

In the present study, we will report a light-activated oxidase-mimic, the fluorine-doped carbon dots (dF-CDs), for the OPD oxidation and finally to the AH determination in Chinese Baijiu. As illustrated in Scheme 1, the as-prepared dF-CDs catalyzed the oxidation of colorless ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). However, the presence of AH inhibited the production of DAP which induce an obvious hypochromic effect and changes of the fluorescence intensity in this system. Therefore, a dual-responsive optical method is established for AH determination, which not only provide a new sensing platform for the AH determination in Baijiu, in particular, extend the applications of CDs to the field of food analysis.

4,5-difluoro-1,2-phenylenediamine (dF-PD), ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD), acetaldehyde (AH), ethyl lactate, ethyl hexanoate, methyl acetate, ethyl caprylate, 1-butanol, 3-methyl-1-butanol, phenethyl alcohol, 2-methyl-1-propanol, ethanol, methanol, formaldehyde, furfural, butyraldehyde, pentanal, 3,3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS), 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetatewere (DCFH-DA), 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. of Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium azide (NaN3) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. (Tianjin, China). Isopropanol was bought from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. (Tianjin, China). The samples of Baijiu were obtained from Luzhou Laojiao Group Co., LTD. (Luzhou, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity), produced by Changchun Ultra-Pure Technology Co., Ltd. (Changchun, China), was used in the whole experiments.

Fluorescence (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) and UVvis absorption (UV-3600, PerkinElmer Inc., Massachusetts, USA) spectrophotometers were used to record the fluorescence data and absorption data. JEM-2200FS equipment (Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used for obtaining transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with a voltage of 200 kV for acceleration. A Thermo Fischer ESCALAB 250Xi was used for XPS analysis using an Al K-Alpha X-ray source (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). An FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., Livingston, UK) was applied to collect time-resolved fluorescence spectra. Gas chromatography of Agilent 7890 A was used to measure the AH content of Baijiu (Agilent Technologies Inc., State of California, USA). Mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) was obtained on a Waters ZQ2000 mass spectrometer (Waters Co., Milford, USA). High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was performed with Waters 2695 (Waters Co., Milford, USA).

The dF-CDs based on dF-PD were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. In brief, 18.0 mg dF-PD was dissolved in 5.0 mL 2.0 % H2SO4 solution. Then, the above solution was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 8.0 h. The obtained dF-CDs were then filtered through a cylinder membrane (0.22 μm), dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis membrane, and stored at 4 °C for further use. In parallel, the fabrication of OPD-CDs based on OPD was the same as dF-CDs, which will not repeat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric determination of AH
ce:section.ce:para: 40 μL of OPD (10 mM), 40 μL dF-CDs and different amounts of AH were first mixed. Then, the final volume was adjusted to be 1.0 mL by adding HAc-NaAc (pH 3.0, 10 mM) buffer solution. The changes in the absorption and emission spectra (λex = 380 nm) were recorded after UV light (365 nm) irradiation for 45 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric determination of AH in Baijiu samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the practical application of the proposed method, the content of AH was measured for 11 Baijiu samples made by different manufacturers, in different aromas and ethanol degrees. For that, dF-CDs (40 μL) and OPD (10 mM, 40 μL) were mixed with different volumes of each Baijiu (2.0–40 μL). The final volume was adjusted to be 1.0 mL by adding HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.0, 10 mM). After being irradiate by UV light (365 nm) for 45 min, the corresponding absorption and emission spectra were recorded respectively.

The oxidase-like activity of dF-CDs was assessed using a redundant substrate of OPD. The oxidation product of 2.3-diaminophenazine (DAP) from OPD is commonly believed to be a dimer, which shows yellow colored reflecting the ongoing level of oxidative reaction. Of note, the widely used CDs originated from OPD (OPD-CDs) show similar and even better catalytic performance as dF-CDs when using OPD as substrate (Fig. S1). However, at acidic conditions, the optical stability of it is not good as dF-CDs (Fig. S2). In Fig. S2A and C, the fluorescence intensity of OPD-CDs changes largely when pHs changing from 3.0 to 7.5, showing a clear pH-dependent emission property. However, the emission intensity of dF-CDs is less affected by pHs (Fig. S2B, C) in comparison to OPD-CDs, indicating that the doping of fluorine in CDs lead the dF-CDs less sensitive to pH, and consequently will prevent it to be affected by the acidic environment of Baijiu for sensing. Besides, when using OPD as carbon resource to fabricate CDs, the excessive feeding material cannot be eliminated thoroughly from the final product of CDs even after several round purification, which will be transferred into DAP either and consequently deviate the quantitation of AH based on DAP signal. Afterward, the stability of the dF-CDs was also explored depending on temperature, salinity, and irradiation of ultraviolet lamp, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3A, after being stored at −20 °C to 80 °C respectively, the fluorescence intensity of dF-CDs has almost no change. While, 70 mins irradiation of ultraviolet light induces only a slight quenching of the dF-CDs intensity (Fig. S3B), and almost non-effect of salinity on its emission (Fig. S3C). Taken together with the faint pH effect on it (Fig. S2), it indicates that the dF-CDs are quite stable under the environments with potential application. Therefore, the dF-CDs will be used as an optical probe in the present study.

The synthetic conditions of dF-CDs were then optimized to achieve better oxidase-like properties. The reaction temperature, lasting time for reaction, and the concentrations of dF-PD as raw materials were monitored respectively based on the light-activated catalytic performance of each product (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S4A and D, the product of DAP reaches a maximum absorption when using dF-CDs originated from 3 mM dF-PD. Besides, the optimal reaction time is selected as 8.0 h (Fig. S4B, E), and the reaction temperature is evaluated to be 180 °C (Fig. S4C, F), according to the catalytic performance of the obtained dF-CDs in each case. Therefore, the dF-CDs with relatively higher oxidase-like properties are achieved at a concentration of 3 mM dF-PD as raw materials, being reacted at 180 °C for 8.0 h.

TEM image was used to characterize the morphology of the optimized dF-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1A, dF-CDs are uniform spheres being well-dispersed in solution, with a mean diameter of 2.04 nm. Then, FT-IR spectra was used to identify the functional groups in dF-CDs (Fig. 1B). The characteristic peaks at 3112 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching model of N−H [23], while those at 1398 cm−1 and 1287 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending model of C−H and C−N, respectively [24]. Besides, the absorption peaks at 1061 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1 is individually attributed to the stretching model of C−F and C−O, respectively [25]. Therefore, the combined use of TEM image and FT-IR spectra proves that the ultra-small dF-CDs are successfully synthesized, possessing abundant hydrophilic groups as −NH2 and C−F at surface.

Subsequently, XPS was used to study the chemical compositions in dF-CDs (Fig. 1C). As summarized in Table S1, the atomic content of C, N, O, and F elements in dF-CDs is proven to be 40.4 %, 17.2 %, 40.2 % and 2.2 %, respectively. Specifically, the C1s spectrum (Fig. S5A) can be deconvoluted into three significant peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 287.4 eV, being assigned for the groups of C−N/C−C, C−O, and C−F, respectively [23–25]. Besides, the N1s spectrum (Fig. S5B) reveals two peaks at 399.9 and 401.7 eV, which is ascribed to pyrrolic N and amino N, respectively [25]. Moreover, the O1s spectrum (Fig. S5C) is deconvoluted into three main peaks at 531.3, 532.6, and 533.2 eV, respectively [25,26], demonstrating the CO, C−O and OC−O groups are contained in the dF-CDs. In particular, the F1s spectrum is finally split into two main bands at 686.7 and 687.3 eV (Fig. S5D), which possibly be an association with semi-ionic C−F and covalent C−F [25]. Therefore, the abundant functional groups as −NH2 and C−F are proposed at the surface of dF-CDs. Such surface functionalities will directly influence the photocatalytic behavior of dF-CDs as which can improve the charge carrier separation, prolong the excited-state lifetime of CDs, as well as promote the interaction of CDs with specific substrates [27,28].

The optical properties of dF-CDs were then investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. In Fig. 1D, an obvious absorption band located at 275 nm is assigned to the π−π* transition of CC bond [23]. The presence of it indicate the conjugation of dF-PD under heating, therefore, the successful synthesis of the carbon core [29]. Besides, the emission peak of dF-CDs does not move with the shift of excitation wavelength, indicating dF-CDs is quite stable and non-excitation dependent. Such excitation-independent fluorescence behavior suggests that the emission of the dF-CDs is essentially rely on the surface states, and which are rather uniform [30]. Meanwhile, the maximum emission peak is shown at approximate 455 nm when being excited at 380 nm, illustrating an optimum excitation wavelength at 380 nm for dF-CDs in aqueous solution.

A typical chromogenic reaction using OPD as substrate was employed to evaluate the light-activated oxidase-mimic activity of dF-CDs. As revealed in Fig. 2A, the optimized dF-CDs show certain improvement on OPD oxidation after exposing the solution to 365 nm for 55 min, which led to the generation of DAP showing an absorption band centered at 450 nm. Setting as control groups, the solutions of OPD with or without UV irradiation, and that of dF-CDs/OPD without light activation, were either test in parallel. Neither of them showed an obvious absorption at 450 nm, indicating the co-contribution of dF-CDs and light irradiation to the OPD oxidation. Besides, Fig. 2B shows that when using another two colorimetric substrates, TMB and ABTS, which can be oxidized either by dF-CDs under 365 nm irradiation, confirming the dF-CDs indeed possess oxidase-like ability under the stimulation of proper light. Besides, to further validate the oxidase-mimic activity of dF-CDs under light, the real-time regulation of A 450 was achieved by keeping switching-on and -off the UV light (365 nm) repeatedly (Fig. S6).

In following, the light-activated oxidase of dF-CDs at different conditions were systematically studied. As the pH of solution, the concentrations of substrate, and the time of light-stimulation are the key factors to directly influence the catalytic process, they were explored by using OPD as substrate (Fig. S7). As shown, the A 450 achieves maximum value at pH 5.5; while it increases rapidly with the concentration increase of OPD, and getting almost saturate at 600 μM after exposure to light for 55 min. Therefore, a solution with pH 5.5, under a light irradiation for 55 min, in the presence of 600 μM OPD are choosed for the following assay.

The steady-state parameters of dF-CDs with light irradiation were then evaluated based on the kinetic analysis [31]. The Michaelise-Menten curves and Lineweaver-Burk plots were displayed in Fig. 2C and D. The calculated steady-state kinetic parameters K
m was 0.39 mM and the maximal initial rate V
m was 11.86 × 10−8 M s−1. Such values were comparable to the reported oxidase-mimics summarized in Table S2, which demonstrate that the developed dF-CDs possess a good binding affinity with the substrate of OPD, and excellent oxidase-mimic property under 365 nm irradiation.

Albeit the intrinsic mechanisms of the CDs-based photo-catalysts have not been fully explored, several kinds of mechanism have been proposed by previous researchers essentially [32,33]. In general, the processes of CDs-based photo-catalysis undergo three stages: (1) the light irradiation results in the generation of electron-hole pairs in CDs; (2) the separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs are good for the formation of reactive species; while the rapid recombination of them severely limits the photocatalytic activity; (3) the subsequent photocatalytic reactions are caused by the generated reactive species. Therefore, such light-activated oxidase-mimic performance was proposed being essentially attributed to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), as being verified in following. Fig. 2E demonstrate that the light-activated oxidase of dF-CDs is significantly inhibited by saturated N2 in solution, which confirms that the oxygen in air is indispensable for such catalytic reaction. In addition, experiments to verify the species of ROS were conducted. For example, SOD, isopropyl alcohol, and NaN3 were used respectively to catch the superoxide radicals (O2 -.), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and singlet oxygen (1O2) (Fig. 2F). The presence of NaN3 or SOD in the system induces obvious decreases of A 450 in comparison to the testing of blank, which indicate that the 1O2 and O2 -. are the main ROS generated by the light-activation of dF-CDs. To better verify the type of active intermediates, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum was carried out (Fig. S8). When using TEMP and DMPO as trapping agents, two distinct peak sets with intensity ratio of 1:1:1 (Figs. S8A) and 1:1:1:1 (Fig. S8B) are shown, being attributed to the specie of 1O2 and O2 -., respectively. However, without light irradiation, no peak is shown. Therefore, O2 -. and 1O2 are recognized as the dominant ROS produced by dF-CDs under the irradiation of UV light, which contributed to the light-activated oxidase performance on OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-responsive optical model of dF-CDs/OPD for AH determination
ce:section.ce:para:
DAP shows an absorption peak at 450 nm; however, the intensity of it decreases largely in the presence of AH (Fig. 2A). Besides, as shown in Fig. S9 for the emission spectra, before adding AH to the sensing system, the emission of dF-CDs at 455 nm is quenched accompanying with the generation of yellow emission at 577 nm for DAP; however, after adding AH to the sensing system, the emission of DAP at 577 nm is quenched; however, the blue emission at 455 nm increases at the same time (Fig. S9). Therefore, both the UV–vis absorption intensity changes at 450 nm (ΔA
450 ) and the emission intensity ratio of I 577/I 455 correlate with the AH concentrations presented in the system, which lay the foundation to develop a dual-responsive optical method for AH determination in solution.

In this work, the quenching mechanism may be caused by inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or electron transfer (ET). Fig. 3A shows considerable overlap between the emission spectrum of dF-CDs and the UV absorption band of DAP centered at 450 nm. Accordingly, both the IFE and FRET [34] are possibly to induce fluorescence quenching of dF-CDs. To clarify it, the emission lifetimes of dF-CDs, and that of it in the presence of 600 μM OPD, are measured (Fig. 3B). There is almost no difference exist between them, and therefore, denied a FRET-induced quenching [35]. Besides, both the positive Zeta-potentials of them (Fig. S10) either excluded the possible adsorption of DAP at the surface of dF-CDs via electrostatic interaction. In this case, the FRET between dF-CDs and DAP would be difficulty to occur due to the distance between donor and acceptor required for FRET must be in nanometer scale (1.0–10 nm) [36]. On the other hand, the electrostatic repulsion between dF-CDs and DAP also exclude the ET as the dominant quenching mechanism. Therefore, the IFE between dF-CDs and DAP is suggested contributing to the fluorescence quenching of dF-CDs.

Then the catalytic conditions, such as pH values of solution, OPD concentrations, and reaction time were optimized for the AH determination (Fig. S11). As shown, the absorbance gap between dF-CDs/OPD and dF-CDs/OPD/AH system is most extensive at pH 3.0 (Fig. S11A, D). In addition, the gap between them reaches the maximum when OPD concentration is 400 μM (Fig. S11B, E), and reaction last for 45 min (Fig. S11C, F), respectively. Therefore, 400 μM OPD, and 45 min of reaction are chosen for AH determination, at a buffer with pH 3.0.

Therefore, at the conditions evaluated above, both the colorimetric and fluorescent determination of AH were conducted. Fig. 4A shows that the absorbance at 450 nm decreases with the adding of more AH. Fig. 4B shows that the absorbance differences at 450 nm (ΔA/A 0 = (A 0 –A)/A 0, where A and A 0 represents the absorbance at 450 nm of the sensing system with and without AH, respectively) changes linearly with AH concentrations in a wide range (10–80 μM), followed by a regression equation of ΔA/A 0 = 0.082+0.0010 [AH] (R
2
= 0.998). Accordingly, a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.68 μM is achieved for AH determination, at the present conditions. In addition, the fluorescence intensity at 455 nm increases while that at 577 nm decreases along with AH additions (Fig. 4C), resulting in a linear response of the relative intensity ratio (I 577/I 455) to AH concentrations (Fig. 4D). In the range of 10–80 μM, the linear regression equation of I 577/I 455 = 2.83–0.030[AH] (R
2
= 0.998), and a LOD of 1.25 μM are achieved for AH, based on the ratiometric fluorescence responses. Furthermore, we compared the newly developed dual-responsive optical method with other reported ones for AH determination (Table S3). As illustrated, it shows relatively lower LOD than most of the previously reported assays for AH. In particular, the proposed sensing system is simple, easy to operate, and the phenomenon is intuitive and visible to the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of dF-CDs/OPD system to AH
ce:section.ce:para: We then investigated the possible mechanism of dF-CDs/OPD system sensing for AH in detail. First, as shown in Fig. 3C and Fig. S12A, the mixing of dF-CDs and AH in solution does not induce the emission changes of dF-CDs, excluding the direct chemical reaction or weak interaction between them. Thus, it is possibly that AH consumed DAP generated in the system, and consequently weakened the absorption and emission signals presented. Such suppose is quickly denied by the fact that the emission ratio of I 577/I 455 remains unchanged when the AH was further added to the finished solution of dF-CDs/OPD (Fig. 3C and Fig. S12B).

Therefore, the possible reasons then move to the less generation of DAP in the system in the presence of AH. For that, three possibility are supposed: the competitive consumption by AH of either light-produced ROS or the material of OPD, or both together in the sensing system. As a weak redundant, AH is possibly reacted with ROS being generated by dF-CDs under irradiation. So, a ROS test is conducted using DCFH-DA as an indicator. The non-fluorescent DCFH-DA can be easily oxidized by ROS to the fluorescent 2,7-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) showing a strong emission at 520 nm. Therefore, the emission intensity at 520 nm (I
520 ) is often used an indicator to evaluate the ROS level presented in the system [37]. As shown in Fig. S13, under 365 nm irradiation, the participation of AH into the solution containing dF-CDs and DCFH-DA does not change the I 520 much, indicating no consumption of ROS by AH directly in the system. Besides, OPD was reported to react with AH at acidic conditions to form 2-methylbenzimidazole [38], the competitive consumption of OPD by AH in the sensing system was then tested. For that, the products of dF-CDs/OPD/AH in the HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=3.0) was then identified by the liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS; Fig. 3D), th peak at 133.05 is ascribed to 2-methylbenzimidazole, corroborating the proposed mechanism exactly. Therefore, the less DAP generation in the system is induced directly by the competitive consumption of OPD by the AH.

In addition, several other species of aldehyde, such as formaldehyde, butyraldehyde, pentanal, and furfural, that may react with OPD were also investigated at the identical sensing conditions as AH. As shown in Fig. S14, the ΔA/A 0 and the emission ratio of I 577/I 455 both change with varying concentration of them, which not only prove the universality of the development method for other aldehyde detection but also further corroborate the proposed competitive mechanism of aldehyde to OPD. Therefore, we conclude that it is the reaction between AH and OPD, in forming the 2-methylbenzimidazole (Fig. 3D), reduces the oxidation products of OPD, which consequently diminishing both the absorbance at 450 nm and the emission intensity ratio of I 577/I 455.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific response of dF-CDs to AH
ce:section.ce:para:
Specificity and interference resistance are important factors for evaluating the feasibility of technique in practice. In order to realize the real determination of AH in Chinese Baijiu, some ingredients were selected as distractors to evaluate the newly developed method. For the colorimetric assay, Fig. S15A, B display that only AH triggers a significant decrease of A
450 , while neither of the other substances have impact on the absorption intensity. Meanwhile, when the above-mentioned substances were added to the dF-CDs/OPD/AH system in 1 equiv. of AH, they have no effect on the absorption intensity of dF-CDs/OPD/AH system either (Fig. S15C, D).

In parallel, for the fluorometric assay, dF-CDs/OPD only responds to AH, while other substances do not cause significant changes in the intensity ratio of fluorescence (Fig. 4E, F), indicating that the fluorometric method possesses high selectivity either. In addition, no obvious fluorescence intensity ratio variations can be observed after adding each substance together with AH into the dF-CDs/OPD system (Fig. S16A, B), manifesting that the proposed method shows satisfactory anti-interference ability for the determination of AH. Therefore, the above results indicate that the dual-responsive methods are highly selective and effective toward AH determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AH in Chinese Baijiu
ce:section.ce:para: In order to extend the AH determination in real samples of Chinese Baijiu, the effect of ethanol on the sensing system should be explored firstly. In order to mimic the alcohol contents of common Chinese Baijiu, three ethanol-water solution (E-Ws) with volume concentrations of 42.0 vol%, 53.0 vol%, 65.8 vol%, were prepared. Upon adding each in different volumes, the absorbance of DAP at 450 nm just fluctuates slightly (Fig. S17), indicating that ethanol has negligible influence on the optical response of the established system. Therefore, the proposed method was then extended for the AH determination in Chinese Baijiu, based on the working plots evaluated in above. Scheme 1 depicts the processes for AH quantification. Before that, the following issues need to pay attention. (1) Only small volume (2.0–40 μL) of Baijiu sample is needed for AH determination because of the high sensitivity of the developed method; (2) As shown in Fig. S14, besides AH both the colorimetry and fluorescence methods respond to formaldehyde, butyraldehyde, and pentanal either. However, in considering the quite low contents of them in commercially available Baijiu of China [2], the effect of them to the AH measurement will be ignored. (3) In addition, although the high content of furfural in Baijiu cannot be ignored, the less sensitive response of the developed method to it made us ignore the furfural content. As presented in Table 1, the recovery rates of AH determined by fluorescence method are located between 97.45–109.76 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are evaluated within 1.23–4.12 % based on the results of five replicates (n = 5) for each sample. In parallel, the recoveries of AH determined by colorimetry method are 97.72–109.67 %, and the corresponding RSD (n = 5) are between 1.09–4.51 %. The good replicates and high consistence with GC results indicate that the content of AH in Chinese Baijiu with different ethanol degrees, and different aromas can be determined accurately by the developed method.

Finally, Pearson correlation analysis was performed on the achieved data in Table 1 to evaluate the biological replicates of the proposed method. The correlation coefficient between data in the column of GC and the colorimetric assay is 0.9969, while that between the GC results and fluorometric assay is shown in a value of 0.9974. Besides, the correlation between the data of colorimetric and fluorescence assays is also conducted, which results in a value of 0.9943. The high correlation coefficients between them indicate the excellent applicability of the developed dual-responsive optical method in wide range of practical samples.

In summary, a colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence dual-responsive model based on the light-activated oxidase-mimic property of dF-CDs has been proposed, which achieved highly sensitive and convenient AH determination in Chinese Baijiu. For that, the fluorin-containing dF-CDs was prepared by dF-PD, which exhibited brilliant oxidase properties under the irradiation of UV light. The produced active species of 1O2 and O2 -. can catalyze the oxidation of OPD to DAP, which shows both of UV–vis absorption and strong fluorescence. However, such processes were inhibited by the presence of AH, linearly in the range of 10−80 μM. The sensing system delivers high sensitivity with detection limits of 1.25 μM for the ratiometric fluorescence, and 0.68 μM for the colorimetry AH determination. In particular, the constructed light-activated system shows excellent stability and self-calibration function of dual-responsive readout, which can sensitively and accurately determine AH in real samples of Chinese Baijiu. Considering the advantages of rapidity and accuracy of the dual-responsive features, the proposed strategy can be an appealing alternative for the AH determination in Baijiu, and possibly for other alcoholic beverages.

Xin-He Duan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yuqing Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Zhijiu Huang: Supervision. Sha Liu: Supervision. Hong-Wei Li: Supervision, Data curation. Yi Li: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to the financial support of NSFC (Nos. 21875085), Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan Project (No. 20230204040YY), and the Innovation & Open Program of the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136356.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xin-He Duan is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Yuqing Wu in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis of carbon dots and their application on sensing.

Hong-Wei Li is an associate professor in the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Jilin University in 2009. His research interests include the development of fluorescent sensors, the controlled preparation and bioapplication of noble metal nanoclusters.
Yi Li is an employee of China Light Industry Enterprises Investment and Development Association.
Zhijiu Huang is an employee Luzhou Laojiao Group Co., LTD.
Sha Liu is an employee Luzhou Laojiao Group Co., LTD.

Yuqing Wu received her Master’s degree (1995) and Ph.D. (1998) from Jilin University. She worked as a postdoc fellow in Kwansei Gakuin University of Japan in 1998. In 2000, she was approved to an associated professor, and currently, she is a professor of chemistry in Jilin University. Her current research interests are mainly focused on the molecular recognition, assembling, and the related mechanism investigation in biological, medical, and chemical systems, as well as the application and new development of various spectroscopic techniques in concern fields.
